name: Semaphore signals
blurb: Mechanical arms. A red stop arm, or a yellow fishtail distant arm — read the arm by day, the lamp by night.
intro: Semaphore signals are the older mechanical type you meet on absolute-block routes in Train Sim World. A moving arm gives the indication: a red square-ended arm is a stop signal; a yellow fishtail arm is a distant. By day you read the arm; by night a coloured lamp beneath the pivot shows the same message. Almost all GB semaphores are upper-quadrant — the arm rises to clear.

variants.name: Stop signal (home)
variants.blurb: Red arm with a white stripe. Tells you whether you may pass this signal.

aspects.name: On (danger)
aspects.whatItMeans: The arm is horizontal (“on”). You must stop at this signal — the section ahead is not clear for you.
aspects.whatYouDo: Stop at the signal and wait for it to clear.
aspects.safetyInteraction: A stop signal itself is not AWS-fitted — on GB lines AWS is provided only at distant signals, so your on-approach warning comes from the distant for this box, not the stop signal. Higher-risk or junction stop signals may carry TPWS, but many older ones rely on you reading the arm by day and the lamp by night.
aspects.controls: Stop the train. There is nothing to acknowledge on the signal itself.
aspects.lookAlikes: A horizontal red arm is danger; a raised red arm is clear. At night, a red lamp is danger, green is clear.

aspects.name: Off (clear)
aspects.whatItMeans: The arm is raised to about 45° (“off”). You may pass the signal and proceed. At night the lamp shows green.
aspects.whatYouDo: Proceed, obeying the line speed and watching the distant/next signal.
aspects.realWorldNote: Upper-quadrant signals raise the arm to clear; a minority of older lower-quadrant signals instead lower the arm. The game may model either depending on the route.

variants.name: Distant signal
variants.blurb: Yellow fishtail arm with a black chevron. Warns about the stop signals ahead.

aspects.name: On (caution)
aspects.whatItMeans: The yellow arm is horizontal (“on”). Be prepared to stop: at least one stop signal ahead is at danger. At night the lamp shows yellow.
aspects.whatYouDo: Control your speed so you can stop at the first stop signal, which may be at danger.
aspects.safetyInteraction: A distant at caution is exactly the kind of aspect AWS warns you about — expect the AWS horn and acknowledge it.
aspects.controls: Acknowledge AWS if it sounds (default: Q) and brake for the stop signals ahead.
aspects.lookAlikes: A distant arm has a fishtail (notched) end and a black chevron; a stop arm is square-ended with a white stripe. A distant can never tell you to stop — only to be ready to.

aspects.name: Off (clear)
aspects.whatItMeans: The yellow arm is raised (“off”). All the stop signals in the section ahead are clear — you can expect a good run. At night the lamp shows green.
